findings.dominantFinding: The dominant imaging finding is bilateral, heterogeneous enlargement of the parotid glands. The glandular parenchyma is diffusely abnormal, containing scattered enhancing solid nodules and multiple small cystic fluid pockets.
findings.supportingFeatures: Supporting features include similar heterogeneous changes and enlargement within the submandibular glands. There may also be associated reactive-appearing cervical lymphadenopathy throughout the neck.
findings.criticalNegatives: Importantly, there is no evidence of a single, highly aggressive, invasive solid mass with poorly defined margins extending into the deep spaces of the neck. Furthermore, there is no frank destruction of the adjacent mandible or skull base.
findings.influentialFeature: The feature that most influences my thinking is the diffuse, bilateral heterogeneous cystic and solid enlargement of the parotid glands without a discrete aggressive primary tumor.

differentials.primaryDiagnosis: The appearances are most in keeping with Sjögren syndrome, given the bilateral heterogeneous glandular enlargement with scattered enhancing nodules and fluid pockets. This autoimmune condition typically affects middle-aged to elderly women and classically presents with a triad of parotid enlargement, keratoconjunctivitis sicca (dry eyes), and xerostomia (dry mouth).
differentials.principleDifferential: The principle differential would be Lymphoepithelial lesions, however these typically occur in patients with acquired immunodeficiency syndrome (AIDS) and often present with larger, more prominent macroscopic cysts alongside profound cervical lymphadenopathy. Without a relevant clinical history of HIV, Sjögren syndrome is a more likely etiology for diffuse cystic and solid parotid change in a middle-aged female.
differentials.excludeDiagnosis: A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Warthin tumors, given they are the second most common benign parotid neoplasm and can also present as bilateral mixed cystic and solid masses. Missing this would alter the surgical approach, although Warthin tumors classically present as discrete, well-circumscribed masses in middle-aged male smokers rather than a diffuse heterogeneous parenchymal process.

management.priority: From a radiological perspective, the priority is to meticulously evaluate the glands for any dominant, solid, aggressively enlarging mass that could indicate malignant transformation to lymphoma.
management.priorImaging: I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is crucial to identify any rapidly growing solid component or disproportionately enlarging lymph node, as patients with Sjögren syndrome carry a significantly elevated risk of developing non-Hodgkin lymphoma.
management.furtherImaging: I would recommend a dedicated MRI of the neck if only CT or ultrasound has been performed, as this modality is superior here for evaluating the complex cystic and solid composition of the glands and detecting subtle solid lymphomatous deposits.
management.mriSequences: I would specify high-resolution T2-weighted and STIR sequences to clearly define the multiple fluid-filled cysts, alongside pre- and post-contrast T1-weighted sequences to detect scattered enhancing nodules and evaluate for any pathologically enlarged solid lymph nodes.
management.nuclearMedicine: An 18F-FDG PET-CT could be considered if there is a strong clinical or radiological suspicion of malignant transformation to lymphoma, allowing for comprehensive staging of metabolically active disease.
management.intervention: If a dominant solid mass or suspicious cervical lymph node is identified, I would specify an ultrasound-guided core needle biopsy to definitively exclude superimposed lymphoma.
management.mdtDiscussion: This should go to the Head and Neck MDT. My report would clearly document the diffuse bilateral nature of the disease, the involvement of other salivary glands, and specifically highlight the presence or absence of any dominant solid lesions requiring urgent tissue sampling.

vivaAnswer.fullScript:
The dominant imaging finding is bilateral, heterogeneous enlargement of the parotid glands. The glandular parenchyma is diffusely abnormal, containing scattered enhancing solid nodules and multiple small cystic fluid pockets. Supporting features include similar heterogeneous changes and enlargement within the submandibular glands. There may also be associated reactive-appearing cervical lymphadenopathy throughout the neck. Importantly, there is no evidence of a single, highly aggressive, invasive solid mass with poorly defined margins extending into the deep spaces of the neck. Furthermore, there is no frank destruction of the adjacent mandible or skull base. The feature that most influences my thinking is the diffuse, bilateral heterogeneous cystic and solid enlargement of the parotid glands without a discrete aggressive primary tumor.

The appearances are most in keeping with Sjögren syndrome, given the bilateral heterogeneous glandular enlargement with scattered enhancing nodules and fluid pockets. This autoimmune condition typically affects middle-aged to elderly women and classically presents with a triad of parotid enlargement, keratoconjunctivitis sicca (dry eyes), and xerostomia (dry mouth). The principle differential would be Lymphoepithelial lesions, however these typically occur in patients with acquired immunodeficiency syndrome (AIDS) and often present with larger, more prominent macroscopic cysts alongside profound cervical lymphadenopathy. Without a relevant clinical history of HIV, Sjögren syndrome is a more likely etiology for diffuse cystic and solid parotid change in a middle-aged female. A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Warthin tumors, given they are the second most common benign parotid neoplasm and can also present as bilateral mixed cystic and solid masses. Missing this would alter the surgical approach, although Warthin tumors classically present as discrete, well-circumscribed masses in middle-aged male smokers rather than a diffuse heterogeneous parenchymal process.

From a radiological perspective, the priority is to meticulously evaluate the glands for any dominant, solid, aggressively enlarging mass that could indicate malignant transformation to lymphoma. I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is crucial to identify any rapidly growing solid component or disproportionately enlarging lymph node, as patients with Sjögren syndrome carry a significantly elevated risk of developing non-Hodgkin lymphoma. I would recommend a dedicated MRI of the neck if only CT or ultrasound has been performed, as this modality is superior here for evaluating the complex cystic and solid composition of the glands and detecting subtle solid lymphomatous deposits. I would specify high-resolution T2-weighted and STIR sequences to clearly define the multiple fluid-filled cysts, alongside pre- and post-contrast T1-weighted sequences to detect scattered enhancing nodules and evaluate for any pathologically enlarged solid lymph nodes. An 18F-FDG PET-CT could be considered if there is a strong clinical or radiological suspicion of malignant transformation to lymphoma, allowing for comprehensive staging of metabolically active disease. If a dominant solid mass or suspicious cervical lymph node is identified, I would specify an ultrasound-guided core needle biopsy to definitively exclude superimposed lymphoma. This should go to the Head and Neck MDT. My report would clearly document the diffuse bilateral nature of the disease, the involvement of other salivary glands, and specifically highlight the presence or absence of any dominant solid lesions requiring urgent tissue sampling.
